descriptive_long.question: rolls without slipping on the curve $vertpos = curveamp \sin \left( \frac{horizpos}{horizscal}\right)$. How are $horizscal,vertirad,curveamp$ related, given that the ellipse completes one revolution when it traverses one period of the curve?

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
A,B,C,R,S,H>0
\]
be fixed real parameters.  
Define the $2\pi$-periodic parametrisation of the triaxial ellipsoid  
\[
E(\theta,\varphi)\;=\;\bigl(A\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\;
                              B\sin\theta\sin\varphi,\;
                              C\cos\theta\bigr),\qquad (\theta,\varphi)\in\mathbb R^{2},
\]
whose image is  
\[
\widehat E:\qquad\frac{x^{2}}{A^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{B^{2}}+\frac{z^{2}}{C^{2}}=1.
\tag{0}
\]

In $\mathbb R^{3}$ fix the ``egg-crate'' surface  
\[
\Sigma:\qquad z \;=\;H
          +R\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{x}{A}\Bigr)
          +S\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{y}{B}\Bigr).
\tag{1}
\]
All data are of class $C^{\infty}$.

Throughout the motion the ellipsoid will be described by a one-parameter family  
\[
g(t)=\bigl(Q(t),c(t)\bigr)\in SE(3),\qquad 0\le t\le T,
\]
where $Q(t)\in SO(3)$ is the time-dependent body attitude and
$c(t)\in\mathbb R^{3}$ the centre-of-mass position.  
Let
\[
(\theta(t),\varphi(t))\in\mathbb R^{2},\qquad 0\le t\le T,
\]
be $C^{1}$ functions whose derivatives are allowed to vanish and that can be
re-parametrised in time at will (in particular we may require
$\dot\theta$ and $\dot\varphi$ to attain the value $0$ at prescribed instants).

The triple $\bigl(g(t),\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)$ is said to perform a
\emph{rolling of $\widehat E$ on $\Sigma$} provided

(i) (contact point)
\[
P(t):=c(t)+Q(t)\,E\bigl(\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)\;\in\;\Sigma;
\tag{2}
\]

(ii) (no-slip) the velocity of the material point of the ellipsoid that
is in contact with $\Sigma$ vanishes:
\[
\dot c(t)+\dot Q(t)\,E\bigl(\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)=0;
\tag{3}
\]

(iii) (no-twist) the angular velocity
\[
\omega(t)=\bigl(\dot Q(t)Q(t)^{-1}\bigr)^{\vee}\in\mathbb R^{3}
\]
is orthogonal to the common unit normal
\[
n(t):=\frac{Q(t)\,n_{E}\bigl(\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)}
           {\lvert n_{E}(\theta(t),\varphi(t))\rvert}
      \;=\;\pm n_{\Sigma}\bigl(x(t),y(t)\bigr)\quad \text{at }P(t),
\tag{4}
\]
where
\[
n_{E}(\theta,\varphi)=
\Bigl(\tfrac{\sin\theta\cos\varphi}{A^{2}},
      \tfrac{\sin\theta\sin\varphi}{B^{2}},
      \tfrac{\cos\theta}{C^{2}}\Bigr),\qquad
n_{\Sigma}(x,y)=
\frac{\bigl(-f_{x},-f_{y},1\bigr)}
     {\sqrt{1+f_{x}^{2}+f_{y}^{2}}},
\]
with $f(x,y)=H+R\cos(x/A)+S\cos(y/B)$.

During the motion the contact point makes two successive lattice steps
on $\Sigma$.

Stage I ($0\le t\le T_{1}$).  
Fix $\theta(t)\equiv\pi/2$ and let $\varphi(t)$ increase monotonically
from $-\pi$ to $\pi$, while both derivatives are required to satisfy
$\dot\varphi(0)=\dot\varphi(T_{1})=0$.  
In space the contact point stays in the plane $y=y_{0}$ and moves in $x$:
\[
x(0)=x_{0},\qquad x(T_{1})=x_{0}+2\pi A.
\tag{S$_{\!I}$}
\]

Stage II ($T_{1}\le t\le T$).  
Fix $\varphi(t)\equiv\pi$ and let $\theta(t)$ increase monotonically
from $\pi/2$ to $3\pi/2$, again with $\dot\theta(T_{1})=\dot\theta(T)=0$.
The $x$-coordinate is frozen at
$x(t)\equiv x_{1}:=x_{0}+2\pi A$ and
\[
y(T_{1})=y_{0},\qquad y(T)=y_{0}+2\pi B.
\tag{S$_{\!II}$}
\]
(The starting ordinates $x_{0},y_{0}$ are arbitrary.  Taking
$\varphi\equiv\pi$ instead of $0$ is immaterial for the local geometry
but simplifies the $C^{1}$ sewing of the two stages.)

a) Kinematics along the prescribed curves.  

 a$_1$) Show that on Stage I there exists  
\[
\lambda_{I}(\varphi,x)=
\frac{\lvert E_{\varphi}(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\varphi)\rvert}
     {\sqrt{\,1+f_{x}(x,y_{0})^{2}}}
\tag{5}
\]
such that $\dot x(t)=\lambda_{I}\bigl(\varphi(t),x(t)\bigr)\,\dot\varphi(t)$.

 a$_2$) Prove the analogous statement for Stage II:
\[
\lambda_{II}(\theta,y)=
\frac{\lvert E_{\theta}(\theta,\pi)\rvert}
     {\sqrt{\,1+f_{y}(x_{1},y)^{2}}},
\tag{6}
\]
so that $\dot y(t)=\lambda_{II}\bigl(\theta(t),y(t)\bigr)\,\dot\theta(t)$.

b) Using the no-slip condition point-wise together with
$(S_{\!I})$-$(S_{\!II})$, prove the
\emph{metric identities}
\[
B^{2}=A^{2}+R^{2}\qquad\text{and}\qquad
C^{2}=A^{2}+S^{2}.
\tag{7}
\]

c) Conversely, assume that (7) holds.

(i) Stage I.  
Insert (7) into (5) to obtain  
\[
\frac{dx}{d\varphi}
  =\Lambda_{I}(\varphi,x):=
   \frac{\sqrt{A^{2}+R^{2}\cos^{2}\varphi}}
        {\sqrt{\,1+(R^{2}/A^{2})\sin^{2}(x/A)}}.
\tag{8}
\]
Show that for every $x_{0}$ equation (8) admits a unique $C^{1}$ solution
on $[-\pi,\pi]$ satisfying
\[
x(\pi)-x(-\pi)=2\pi A.
\tag{9}
\]

(ii) Stage II.  
Analogously, prove that the solution of  
\[
\frac{dy}{d\theta}
  =\Lambda_{II}(\theta,y):=
   \frac{\sqrt{A^{2}+S^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}}
        {\sqrt{\,1+(S^{2}/B^{2})\sin^{2}(y/B)}}
\tag{10}
\]
satisfies
\[
y(3\pi/2)-y(\pi/2)=2\pi B.
\tag{11}
\]

(iii) Construct a rolling realising Stages I-II and prove that the
resulting rigid motion $g(t)$ is of class $C^{1}$ on $[0,T]$.  In
particular, justify carefully that the body configurations at $t=T_{1}$
match up to a (possibly trivial) \emph{body-fixed} rotation about the
common normal and a $(\theta,\varphi)$ shift leaving the contact point
invariant.  
(You may use that the rolling constraints define a smooth Ehresmann
connection on the bundle $SE(3)\times\widehat E\longrightarrow\widehat
E$ and that the time parametrisation of Stage I can always be slowed
down so that $\dot\varphi(T_{1})=0$, likewise for Stage II.)

(Hints.  
- For (c-i) evaluate the integral
$\displaystyle\int\sqrt{1+k^{2}\sin^{2}u}\,du$ explicitly and exploit
its $\pi$-periodicity.  
- For (c-iii) choose the initial orientation of Stage II equal to the
final orientation of Stage I in order to guarantee $C^{1}$-matching.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  
\[
r(\theta,\varphi):=E(\theta,\varphi),\qquad
f(x,y)=H+R\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{x}{A}\Bigr)+S\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{y}{B}\Bigr),\qquad
P=c+Qr.
\]
All quantities are $C^{\infty}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{a) Kinematics.}

Differentiating $P=c+Qr$ gives
\[
\dot P=\dot c+\dot Q\,r+Q\,\dot r.
\tag{12}
\]
By the no-slip condition $(\dot c+\dot Q\,r)=0$, hence
\[
\dot P=Q\,\dot r.
\tag{13}
\]
Thus the motion of the contact point along $\Sigma$ is governed
\emph{solely} by the variation of $(\theta,\varphi)$.

------------------------------------------------
a$_1$) Stage I $(\theta\equiv\pi/2,\;y\equiv y_{0})$.  
Here
\[
r_{\varphi}\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\varphi\bigr)=
(-A\sin\varphi,\;B\cos\varphi,\;0),\qquad
v_{E}:=r_{\varphi}\,\dot\varphi.
\]
With (13)
\[
\dot P=Qv_{E}=\dot x\,(1,\,0,\,f_{x}(x,y_{0})).
\]
Both vectors lie in the one-dimensional subspace $T_{P}\Sigma$, so
there exists $\lambda_{I}$ with
\[
Qv_{E}=\lambda_{I}(\varphi,x)\bigl(1,\,0,\,f_{x}(x,y_{0})\bigr).
\]
Taking Euclidean norms gives (5); equating first components yields
$\dot x=\lambda_{I}\dot\varphi$.

------------------------------------------------
a$_2$) Stage II $(\varphi\equiv\pi,\;x\equiv x_{1})$.  
Now
\[
r_{\theta}(\theta,\pi)=(-A\cos\theta,\;0,\;-C\sin\theta),\qquad
v_{E}:=r_{\theta}\,\dot\theta.
\]
Proceeding as above we obtain (6) and
$\dot y=\lambda_{II}\dot\theta$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{b) Metric identities.}

Stage I: by (13) and a$_1$
\[
\lvert r_{\varphi}\rvert\,\dot\varphi
   =\sqrt{1+f_{x}^{2}}\;\dot x.
\tag{14}
\]
Since $\dot\varphi,\dot x\ge 0$ no absolute values are needed.
Integrating over $[0,T_{1}]$ and using $(S_{\!I})$ gives
\[
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}
   \lvert r_{\varphi}(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\varphi)\rvert\,d\varphi
=\int_{x_{0}}^{x_{0}+2\pi A}
   \sqrt{1+f_{x}(x,y_{0})^{2}}\,dx.
\tag{15}
\]
The left-hand side equals
\[
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}
   \sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\varphi+B^{2}\cos^{2}\varphi}\,d\varphi,
\]
while the right-hand side equals
\[
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}+R^{2}\sin^{2}u}\,du
\quad\text{with }u:=x/A.
\]

Stage II (interchanging $(x,y),(R,S),(B,C)$) yields
\[
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}+S^{2}\sin^{2}u}\,du
=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\theta+C^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}\,d\theta.
\]

Set
\[
F(b):=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\vartheta+b^{2}\cos^{2}\vartheta}\,d\vartheta,
\quad b>0.
\]
Differentiating under the integral sign gives
\[
F'(b)=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\frac{b\cos^{2}\vartheta}
              {\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\vartheta+b^{2}\cos^{2}\vartheta}}
       \,d\vartheta>0,
\]
so $F$ is strictly increasing.  Equation (15) therefore forces
$B^{2}=A^{2}+R^{2}$, and Stage II yields $C^{2}=A^{2}+S^{2}$, i.e.~(7).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{c) Inverse construction under assumption (7).}

(i) Stage I.  
With $u:=x/A$ and $k:=R/A$ equation (8) becomes
\[
\frac{du}{d\varphi}
   =\Phi(\varphi,u):=
    \frac{\sqrt{1+k^{2}\cos^{2}\varphi}}
         {\sqrt{1+k^{2}\sin^{2}u}}.
\tag{16}
\]
$\Phi$ is $C^{1}$ and strictly positive on the cylinder
$(\varphi,u)\in[-\pi,\pi]\times\mathbb R$, hence the ODE has a unique
$C^{1}$ solution for all $\varphi\in[-\pi,\pi]$.  Separation of
variables gives
\[
\int_{u_{0}}^{u(\varphi)}\sqrt{1+k^{2}\sin^{2}s}\,ds
   =\int_{-\pi}^{\varphi}\sqrt{1+k^{2}\cos^{2}\tau}\,d\tau.
\tag{17}
\]
The integrands are $\pi$-periodic and even, so evaluating at
$\varphi=\pi$ yields $u(\pi)=u_{0}+2\pi$, i.e.~(9).

(ii) Stage II.  
Substituting $v:=y/B$ and $\ell:=S/B$ converts (10) into the same model
equation, proving (11).

(iii) \emph{Existence of a $C^{1}$ rolling realising both stages.}

\emph{Step 1: horizontal lift along Stage I.}  
Choose an arbitrary initial configuration $g(0)=(Q_{0},c_{0})$.
Because the base curve $\bigl(\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)$ and the
contact point on $\Sigma$ are prescribed, the rolling connection
provides a unique horizontal lift $g(t)=(Q(t),c(t))$ on $[0,T_{1}]$.
By construction $\dot\varphi(0)=0$ and we have arranged $\dot\varphi(T_{1})=0$,
so
\[
\dot Q(T_{1})=\dot c(T_{1})=0.
\tag{18}
\]

\emph{Step 2: initial data for Stage II.}  
At $t=T_{1}$ the contact point satisfies
$\theta=\pi/2,\varphi=\pi$ and the common normal is
$n(T_{1})=\tfrac{Q(T_{1})\,n_{E}(\pi/2,\pi)}{\lvert n_{E}(\pi/2,\pi)\rvert}$.
Because for a fixed contact point and normal the admissible body
orientations form a one-parameter family obtained by rotations around
the normal, we \emph{choose}
\[
g(T_{1}^{+})=(Q(T_{1}),c(T_{1})).
\tag{19}
\]
This choice suppresses any jump in $Q$ or $c$.

\emph{Step 3: horizontal lift along Stage II.}  
From the initial data (19) the rolling connection yields a unique
horizontal lift on $[T_{1},T]$.  Because $\dot\theta(T_{1})=0$ the
right-hand sides of the differential equations for $Q$ and $c$ vanish
at $t=T_{1}$, so with (18)
\[
\dot Q(T_{1}^{+})=\dot Q(T_{1}^{-}),\qquad
\dot c(T_{1}^{+})=\dot c(T_{1}^{-}),
\]
and the overall map $g:[0,T]\to SE(3)$ is $C^{1}$.

\emph{Step 4: summary.}  
The curve
$t\mapsto\bigl(g(t),\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)$ meets all rolling
constraints, realises the required lattice steps (thanks to (9) and
(11)), and is of class $C^{1}$ on $[0,T]$.  Consequently the rolling
exists if and only if the metric identities (7) hold.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
A $C^{1}$ rolling of the triaxial ellipsoid \eqref{0} on the egg-crate
surface \eqref{1} performing the two prescribed lattice steps exists
\emph{iff}
\[
\boxed{\;B^{2}=A^{2}+R^{2},\qquad C^{2}=A^{2}+S^{2}\;}.
\qquad\qedhere
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension — The original problem is planar; the enhanced
variant takes place in ℝ³ with a full ellipsoid rolling on a doubly
periodic surface, so one deals with two-parameter families of contact
points instead of a single curve.

2. Additional constraints — Besides equality of arc lengths, the problem
demands second-order tangency (“no twist’’) and *simultaneous* matching
of two independent periodic motions (one about each principal axis of
the ellipsoid).

3. Sophisticated structures — The solution needs first fundamental
forms, differential geometry of surfaces, and monotonicity properties of
complete elliptic integrals rather than elementary arc-length calculus
alone.

4. Deeper theory — Showing that the coincidence of the metrics along two
independent directions suffices for full metric coincidence, and that
horizontal motion of the centre follows from the common normal argument,
requires familiarity with rigid surface rolling theory.

5. Multiple interacting concepts — The argument couples surface geometry
(elliptic integrals, metric tensors) with rigid-body kinematics
(synchronous rotations about two axes and horizontal centre-of-mass
motion).

All these layers make the enhanced kernel variant substantially more
technically demanding than both the original Olympiad-style question and
the current kernel variant.

\[

\tag{0}

\[

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  
\[
r(\theta,\varphi):=E(\theta,\varphi),\qquad
f(x,y)=H+R\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{x}{A}\Bigr)+S\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{y}{B}\Bigr),\qquad
P=c+Qr.
\]
All quantities are $C^{\infty}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{a) Kinematics.}

Differentiating $P=c+Qr$ gives
\[
\dot P=\dot c+\dot Q\,r+Q\,\dot r.
\tag{12}
\]
By the no-slip condition $(\dot c+\dot Q\,r)=0$, hence
\[
\dot P=Q\,\dot r.
\tag{13}
\]
Thus the motion of the contact point along $\Sigma$ is governed
\emph{solely} by the variation of $(\theta,\varphi)$.

------------------------------------------------
a$_1$) Stage I $(\theta\equiv\pi/2,\;y\equiv y_{0})$.  
Here
\[
r_{\varphi}\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\varphi\bigr)=
(-A\sin\varphi,\;B\cos\varphi,\;0),\qquad
v_{E}:=r_{\varphi}\,\dot\varphi.
\]

\[
\dot P=Qv_{E}=\dot x\,(1,\,0,\,f_{x}(x,y_{0})).
\]
Both vectors lie in the one-dimensional subspace $T_{P}\Sigma$, so
there exists $\lambda_{I}$ with
\[
Qv_{E}=\lambda_{I}(\varphi,x)\bigl(1,\,0,\,f_{x}(x,y_{0})\bigr).
\]
Taking Euclidean norms gives (5); equating first components yields
$\dot x=\lambda_{I}\dot\varphi$.

------------------------------------------------
a$_2$) Stage II $(\varphi\equiv\pi,\;x\equiv x_{1})$.  
Now
\[
r_{\theta}(\theta,\pi)=(-A\cos\theta,\;0,\;-C\sin\theta),\qquad
v_{E}:=r_{\theta}\,\dot\theta.
\]
Proceeding as above we obtain (6) and
$\dot y=\lambda_{II}\dot\theta$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{b) Metric identities.}

Stage I: by (13) and a$_1$
\[
\lvert r_{\varphi}\rvert\,\dot\varphi
   =\sqrt{1+f_{x}^{2}}\;\dot x.
\tag{14}
\]
Since $\dot\varphi,\dot x\ge 0$ no absolute values are needed.
Integrating over $[0,T_{1}]$ and using $(S_{\!I})$ gives
\[
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}
   \lvert r_{\varphi}(\tfrac{\pi}{2},\varphi)\rvert\,d\varphi
=\int_{x_{0}}^{x_{0}+2\pi A}
   \sqrt{1+f_{x}(x,y_{0})^{2}}\,dx.
\tag{15}
\]
The left-hand side equals
\[
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}
   \sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\varphi+B^{2}\cos^{2}\varphi}\,d\varphi,
\]
while the right-hand side equals
\[
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}+R^{2}\sin^{2}u}\,du
\quad\text{with }u:=x/A.
\]

Stage II (interchanging $(x,y),(R,S),(B,C)$) yields
\[
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}+S^{2}\sin^{2}u}\,du
=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\theta+C^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}\,d\theta.
\]

Set
\[
F(b):=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\vartheta+b^{2}\cos^{2}\vartheta}\,d\vartheta,
\quad b>0.
\]
Differentiating under the integral sign gives
\[
F'(b)=\int_{0}^{2\pi}\frac{b\cos^{2}\vartheta}
              {\sqrt{A^{2}\sin^{2}\vartheta+b^{2}\cos^{2}\vartheta}}
       \,d\vartheta>0,
\]
so $F$ is strictly increasing.  Equation (15) therefore forces
$B^{2}=A^{2}+R^{2}$, and Stage II yields $C^{2}=A^{2}+S^{2}$, i.e.~(7).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{c) Inverse construction under assumption (7).}

(i) Stage I.  
With $u:=x/A$ and $k:=R/A$ equation (8) becomes
\[
\frac{du}{d\varphi}
   =\Phi(\varphi,u):=
    \frac{\sqrt{1+k^{2}\cos^{2}\varphi}}
         {\sqrt{1+k^{2}\sin^{2}u}}.
\tag{16}
\]
$\Phi$ is $C^{1}$ and strictly positive on the cylinder
$(\varphi,u)\in[-\pi,\pi]\times\mathbb R$, hence the ODE has a unique
$C^{1}$ solution for all $\varphi\in[-\pi,\pi]$.  Separation of
variables gives
\[
\int_{u_{0}}^{u(\varphi)}\sqrt{1+k^{2}\sin^{2}s}\,ds
   =\int_{-\pi}^{\varphi}\sqrt{1+k^{2}\cos^{2}\tau}\,d\tau.
\tag{17}
\]
The integrands are $\pi$-periodic and even, so evaluating at
$\varphi=\pi$ yields $u(\pi)=u_{0}+2\pi$, i.e.~(9).

(ii) Stage II.  
Substituting $v:=y/B$ and $\ell:=S/B$ converts (10) into the same model
equation, proving (11).

(iii) \emph{Existence of a $C^{1}$ rolling realising both stages.}

\emph{Step 1: horizontal lift along Stage I.}  
Choose an arbitrary initial configuration $g(0)=(Q_{0},c_{0})$.
Because the base curve $\bigl(\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)$ and the
contact point on $\Sigma$ are prescribed, the rolling connection
provides a unique horizontal lift $g(t)=(Q(t),c(t))$ on $[0,T_{1}]$.
By construction $\dot\varphi(0)=0$ and we have arranged $\dot\varphi(T_{1})=0$,
so
\[
\dot Q(T_{1})=\dot c(T_{1})=0.
\tag{18}
\]

\emph{Step 2: initial data for Stage II.}  
At $t=T_{1}$ the contact point satisfies
$\theta=\pi/2,\varphi=\pi$ and the common normal is
$n(T_{1})=\tfrac{Q(T_{1})\,n_{E}(\pi/2,\pi)}{\lvert n_{E}(\pi/2,\pi)\rvert}$.
Because for a fixed contact point and normal the admissible body
orientations form a one-parameter family obtained by rotations around
the normal, we \emph{choose}
\[
g(T_{1}^{+})=(Q(T_{1}),c(T_{1})).
\tag{19}
\]
This choice suppresses any jump in $Q$ or $c$.

\emph{Step 3: horizontal lift along Stage II.}  
From the initial data (19) the rolling connection yields a unique
horizontal lift on $[T_{1},T]$.  Because $\dot\theta(T_{1})=0$ the
right-hand sides of the differential equations for $Q$ and $c$ vanish
at $t=T_{1}$, so with (18)
\[
\dot Q(T_{1}^{+})=\dot Q(T_{1}^{-}),\qquad
\dot c(T_{1}^{+})=\dot c(T_{1}^{-}),
\]
and the overall map $g:[0,T]\to SE(3)$ is $C^{1}$.

\emph{Step 4: summary.}  
The curve
$t\mapsto\bigl(g(t),\theta(t),\varphi(t)\bigr)$ meets all rolling
constraints, realises the required lattice steps (thanks to (9) and
(11)), and is of class $C^{1}$ on $[0,T]$.  Consequently the rolling
exists if and only if the metric identities (7) hold.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
A $C^{1}$ rolling of the triaxial ellipsoid \eqref{0} on the egg-crate
surface \eqref{1} performing the two prescribed lattice steps exists
\emph{iff}
\[
\boxed{\;B^{2}=A^{2}+R^{2},\qquad C^{2}=A^{2}+S^{2}\;}.
\qquad\qedhere
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension — The original problem is planar; the enhanced
variant takes place in ℝ³ with a full ellipsoid rolling on a doubly
periodic surface, so one deals with two-parameter families of contact
points instead of a single curve.

2. Additional constraints — Besides equality of arc lengths, the problem
demands second-order tangency (“no twist’’) and *simultaneous* matching
of two independent periodic motions (one about each principal axis of
the ellipsoid).

3. Sophisticated structures — The solution needs first fundamental
forms, differential geometry of surfaces, and monotonicity properties of
complete elliptic integrals rather than elementary arc-length calculus
alone.

4. Deeper theory — Showing that the coincidence of the metrics along two
independent directions suffices for full metric coincidence, and that
horizontal motion of the centre follows from the common normal argument,
requires familiarity with rigid surface rolling theory.

5. Multiple interacting concepts — The argument couples surface geometry
(elliptic integrals, metric tensors) with rigid-body kinematics
(synchronous rotations about two axes and horizontal centre-of-mass
motion).

All these layers make the enhanced kernel variant substantially more
technically demanding than both the original Olympiad-style question and
the current kernel variant.
